attributes.stem: A 16-year-old girl presents to the Emergency Department with symptoms of pneumonia. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. Her immunisations are up to date. On examination, she appears unwell with a respiratory rate of 28/min, oxygen saturation of 92% on room air, and crackles in her right lower lung field. She is diagnosed with community-acquired pneumonia and given an intravenous injection of penicillin. Within minutes, she develops hypotension and exhibits difficulty with ventilation. She is immediately resuscitated and stabilised.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following tests would be most supportive of a diagnosis of anaphylaxis if performed immediately following resuscitation?
attributes.explanation:
This case describes a severe, immediate hypersensitivity reaction to penicillin, consistent with anaphylaxis. The patient developed hypotension and respiratory distress within minutes of receiving intravenous penicillin, which are classic signs of an anaphylactic reaction. In such cases, prompt diagnosis and management are crucial.

Serum tryptase is the most specific laboratory test for diagnosing anaphylaxis. Tryptase is a protease released by mast cells during anaphylaxis. Its levels peak about 1-2 hours after the onset of symptoms and can remain elevated for several hours. Therefore, measuring serum tryptase immediately following resuscitation would be most supportive of a diagnosis of anaphylaxis.

Serum histamine levels also rise during anaphylaxis but peak within 5-10 minutes of symptom onset and return to baseline within 30-60 minutes. This makes histamine levels less reliable than tryptase, especially if there's any delay in obtaining the sample.

The penicillin radioallergosorbent test (RAST) detects specific IgE antibodies to penicillin. While this test can be useful in diagnosing penicillin allergy, it is not helpful in the acute setting of suspected anaphylaxis. RAST testing is typically performed weeks after the event to confirm the presence of penicillin-specific IgE.

C1-esterase inhibitor level is used to diagnose hereditary angio-oedema, not anaphylaxis. While angio-oedema can occur in anaphylaxis, C1-esterase inhibitor deficiency causes a different type of angio-oedema that is not associated with urticaria or other systemic symptoms of anaphylaxis.

Serum elastase is not typically used in the diagnosis of anaphylaxis. It is more commonly associated with conditions like pancreatitis or neutrophil-mediated inflammation.

In this case, the clinical presentation strongly suggests anaphylaxis to penicillin. While the diagnosis of anaphylaxis is primarily clinical, serum tryptase can provide valuable supporting evidence, especially in cases where the diagnosis might be uncertain. It's important to note that normal tryptase levels do not rule out anaphylaxis, particularly in food-induced reactions. The patient should be referred to an allergist for further evaluation and management, including consideration of skin testing and oral challenges if appropriate, to confirm the penicillin allergy.
learningPoints: Serum tryptase is the most specific laboratory test for diagnosing anaphylaxis, particularly when measured within 1-3 hours of symptom onset.
Anaphylaxis to penicillin typically presents with rapid-onset systemic symptoms including hypotension and respiratory distress.
Immediate hypersensitivity reactions to penicillin are IgE-mediated and can be life-threatening, requiring prompt recognition and treatment.
references: National Clinical Guideline Centre (UK). Drug Allergy: Diagnosis and Management of Drug Allergy in Adults, Children and Young People. London: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE); 2014 Sep. (NICE Clinical Guidelines, No. 183.) 7, Measuring serum tryptase after suspected anaphylaxis. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK274147/
